title: Needful Things - The Ran(King) of King - Part 4
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=qbXC7hLKuGM

Daniel (@dungeondive) tackles 1991's Needful Things in The Ran(King) of King. Leland Gaunt opens a curious shop in Castle Rock that happens to stock the one item each customer most desires — for a little money and a small "favour." Those favours are pranks Gaunt sets neighbour against neighbour, escalating the town's buried greed and resentment toward a violent, town-wide breakdown.

Daniel reads it not as horror but as bombastic dark satire on consumerism — a farce, and a decent one, though he wryly notes the irony of a man who sells things writing a sledgehammer critique of wanting things (gesturing at his own wall of games). He loves the concept and singles out two strong protagonists, Sheriff Alan Pangborn and Polly Chalmers, as among his favourite King leads, and praises a genuinely excellent final fifth where the whole town erupts into knife-fights, dynamite and chaos — much like The Tommyknockers' climax (he muses on the two as flip sides of "evil from within" versus "evil from outside").

But the plot can't carry nearly 800 pages: the middle third is repetitive enough that he skimmed it, which tells against the book. It drops to the bottom of the board.

----

Does Needful Things work better as satire than horror for you — and is its bloated middle a fatal flaw or a forgivable one?
